g.data-narration: Welcome to Computer Vision Fundamentals. Today we explore digital images - the foundation of all visual computing.
A digital image is a 2D function f(x,y) where x,y are coordinates and f is the intensity at each point - like a mathematical map of brightness.
g.text: Definition: A digital image is a 2D function f(x, y) where
x, y are spatial coordinates and f(x,y) is the intensity at that location.
Mathematical representation: I(x,y) = intensity value at position (x,y)

g.data-narration: Digitization has two steps: sampling discretizes spatial coordinates, while quantization discretizes intensity values into finite levels.
Pixels are the smallest addressable units in an image grid. Each pixel has a location (x,y) and a value representing intensity or color.
g.text: Highlighted pixel at position (1,1) with intensity value

g.data-narration: Spatial resolution is the total pixel count (like 1920×1080), while intensity resolution is bit depth determining color levels (8-bit = 256 levels).
Binary images use just 1 bit per pixel (0 or 1), while grayscale uses 8 bits for 256 intensity levels from black to white.
RGB uses three 8-bit channels (Red, Green, Blue) totaling 24 bits per pixel, enabling 16.7 million possible colors.
g.text: 24 bits per pixel (8 bits × 3 channels) = 16.7 million colors
R channel | G channel | B channel

g.data-narration: The grayscale formula weights green highest (0.587) because human eyes are most sensitive to green, followed by red (0.299), then blue (0.114).
Different color spaces serve different purposes: RGB for displays, HSV for intuitive color editing, YCbCr for compression, LAB for perceptually uniform matching.
HSV separates color type (Hue 0-360°), purity (Saturation 0-100%), and brightness (Value 0-100%), making it intuitive for humans.
YCbCr separates luminance (Y) from chrominance (Cb, Cr), enabling efficient compression. LAB is perceptually uniform - equal distance equals equal perceived difference.
g.text: JPEG, MPEG, H.264 video compression use this

g.data-narration: Images are stored as matrices: grayscale is M×N, color is M×N×3. The origin (0,0) is at top-left with y-axis pointing downward.
File size formula: width × height × bits_per_pixel ÷ 8. A Full HD RGB image needs about 6MB uncompressed.
